title: List files by size
prompt: Write a single Linux command to list all files in the current directory sorted by size, largest first, in long-listing format.
explanation: `ls -l` gives long format (permissions, owner, size, date); `-S` sorts by file size descending. This is the fastest built-in way to spot large files without piping to `sort`.

title: Find recently modified log files
prompt: Write a command to find all `.log` files under `/var/log` that were modified in the last 24 hours.
explanation: `-mtime -1` matches files modified less than 1 day ago (the `-` means "less than"). `-name '*.log'` restricts the search to log files by extension.

title: Count lines in a file
prompt: Given a file `access.log`, write a command to print the number of lines it contains.
explanation: `wc` (word count) with `-l` counts newline characters, which for a normal text file equals the number of lines.

title: Make a script executable
prompt: You have a file `deploy.sh` that only the owner should be able to execute (in addition to existing read/write). Write the command to add that permission without changing group/other permissions.
explanation: `u+x` adds the execute bit only for the file's owner (`u`), leaving group (`g`) and other (`o`) permissions untouched — safer than `chmod 755` if you don't know the existing bits.

title: Case-insensitive error search
prompt: Write a command to search `app.log` for the word "error" regardless of case (matches ERROR, Error, error, etc.) and print matching lines with their line numbers.
explanation: `-i` makes the match case-insensitive, `-n` prefixes each match with its line number, which is essential when you need to jump straight to the line in an editor.

title: Top memory-consuming processes
prompt: Write a command that lists the top 5 processes by memory usage (RSS), most memory-hungry first.
explanation: `ps aux` lists all processes with resource columns; `--sort=-%mem` sorts descending by percent-memory column. `head -6` keeps the header row plus the top 5 processes.

title: Delete stale temp files safely
prompt: Write a single command that finds all regular files under `/tmp` older than 7 days and deletes them, without deleting directories.
explanation: `-type f` restricts to regular files so directories are untouched. `-mtime +7` means "more than 7 days old". `-delete` is safer and faster than piping to `xargs rm` because `find` handles the removal internally without spawning a shell per file.

title: Top IP addresses from an access log
prompt: Given `access.log` where the first whitespace-separated field is the client IP, write a pipeline that prints each unique IP with its hit count, sorted from most to least frequent.
explanation: `awk '{print $1}'` extracts the IP column. `sort` groups identical IPs together so `uniq -c` can count consecutive duplicates. The final `sort -rn` orders the counts numerically, descending.

title: Schedule a nightly backup
prompt: Write the crontab line to run `/home/ops/backup.sh` every day at 2:30 AM.
explanation: Crontab fields are `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`. `30 2 * * *` fires at minute 30 of hour 2 (2:30 AM), every day of every month, every day of the week.

title: In-place find and replace
prompt: Write a command to replace every occurrence of `foo` with `bar` in `config.txt`, editing the file in place and keeping a `.bak` backup of the original.
explanation: `-i.bak` edits the file in place while saving the pre-edit version as `config.txt.bak`. The `s/foo/bar/g` substitution command replaces every match on every line (`g` = global, not just the first per line).

title: Free up a port held by a stuck process
prompt: Write commands to find which process is listening on TCP port 8080 and terminate it gracefully.
explanation: `lsof -i :8080` lists the process bound to that port along with its PID. `kill -15` sends SIGTERM, requesting a clean shutdown, which should always be tried before the forceful `kill -9`.

title: Compress a directory excluding one folder
prompt: Write a command to create `www.tar.gz` from the `/var/www` directory while excluding the `node_modules` folder anywhere inside it.
explanation: `--exclude` skips any path matching the pattern during archiving. `-c` creates, `-z` gzips, `-f` names the output file. `-C /var/www .` archives the directory's contents without embedding the full absolute path in the tarball.

title: Sum a column grouped by category
prompt: Given a comma-separated file `sales.csv` with columns `region,amount`, write an awk one-liner that prints each region with the sum of its amounts.

correctAnswer.solution: awk -F, '{sum[$1]+=$2} END {for (r in sum) print r, sum[r]}' sales.csv

explanation: `-F,` sets the field separator to a comma. `sum[$1]+=$2` accumulates the amount into an associative array keyed by region as each line is read. The `END` block runs once after all lines are processed, iterating the array to print totals.

title: Disk usage alert script
prompt: Write a bash script that checks root filesystem usage and prints "ALERT: disk usage above 90%" (and exits with status 1) if usage exceeds 90%, otherwise prints "OK".

correctAnswer.solution:
#!/bin/bash
USAGE=$(df / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '%')
if [ "$USAGE" -gt 90 ]; then
  echo "ALERT: disk usage above 90%"
  exit 1
else
  echo "OK"
fi

explanation: `df /` reports root filesystem stats; `tail -1` drops the header, `awk '{print $5}'` grabs the use-percentage column, and `tr -d '%'` strips the `%` so it can be compared numerically with `-gt` in the `if` test.

title: Recursively chown files safely with spaces in names
prompt: Write a command to recursively change ownership of all `.conf` files under `/etc/app` to `appuser:appgroup`, safely handling filenames that contain spaces.
explanation: `-print0` separates found paths with a null byte instead of whitespace/newlines, and `xargs -0` reads that same null-delimited stream — the only combination that is fully safe for filenames containing spaces, tabs, or newlines.

title: Create a symbolic link
prompt: Write a command to create a symbolic link named `current` pointing to `/opt/releases/v2.3`.
explanation: `ln -s target link_name` creates a symlink. The target is the real path being pointed to; the second argument is the new link's name — commonly used for zero-downtime "current release" pointers in deployments.

title: Directory size summary
prompt: Write a command to show the total disk usage of `/var/log` in human-readable form (not a per-file breakdown).
explanation: `-s` summarizes into a single total instead of listing every subdirectory, and `-h` formats the size as KB/MB/GB instead of raw byte counts.

title: Check and restart a service
prompt: Write commands to check the status of the `nginx` service and restart it.

correctAnswer.solution:
systemctl status nginx
systemctl restart nginx

explanation: `systemctl` is the standard interface to systemd-managed services on modern Linux; `status` shows whether it's running plus recent log lines, and `restart` stops then starts it in one command.

title: Tail live logs
prompt: Write a command to continuously follow new lines being appended to `/var/log/app/app.log` in real time.
explanation: `-f` (follow) keeps `tail` running and prints new lines as they're written, which is the standard way to watch a log during a deployment or while reproducing a bug.

title: Recursive grep across a directory
prompt: Write a command to search recursively through `/etc/app` for any file containing the string "DEBUG_MODE".
explanation: `-r` makes grep descend into subdirectories automatically instead of only checking files in the top-level path given.

title: Diff two config files
prompt: Write a command to show the differences between `config.old.yml` and `config.new.yml`.
explanation: `diff` compares two files line by line and prints only the lines that differ, prefixed with `<` for the first file and `>` for the second — the basic building block behind most patch/version-control diffing.

title: List processes with open network connections
prompt: Write a command to list all processes with active TCP connections along with the process name and PID.
explanation: `ss` is the modern replacement for `netstat`; `-t` filters to TCP, `-n` shows numeric addresses/ports instead of resolving hostnames (much faster), and `-p` shows the owning process name and PID.

title: Extract a column from /etc/passwd
prompt: Write a command that lists just the usernames from `/etc/passwd` (a colon-delimited file where the username is the first field).
explanation: `-F:` sets the field separator to a colon to match `/etc/passwd`'s format, and `{print $1}` prints only the first field of each line.

title: Tee output to a file and the screen
prompt: Write a command that runs `./deploy.sh`, showing its output on screen while also saving it to `deploy.log`.
explanation: `tee` reads from stdin and writes to both stdout and the named file simultaneously. `2>&1` redirects stderr into stdout first, so both error and normal output get captured in the log, not just stdout.

title: Sync a directory to a remote server
prompt: Write a command to sync the local `/var/www/site/` directory to `user@server:/var/www/site/`, only transferring changed files and deleting remote files no longer present locally.
explanation: `-a` (archive) preserves permissions/timestamps/symlinks, `-v` is verbose, `-z` compresses data in transit, and `--delete` removes files on the destination that no longer exist on the source — without it, stale files would accumulate remotely.

title: Set exact permissions with octal notation
prompt: Write a command to set `deploy_key.pem` so only the owner can read or write it (no execute, no access for group/other).
explanation: Octal 600 breaks down as owner=rw- (6), group=--- (0), other=--- (0). SSH and similar tools will actually refuse to use a private key file if its permissions are looser than this.

title: Create a persistent alias
prompt: Write the line you'd add to `~/.bashrc` to create a permanent alias `ll` for `ls -alF`.
explanation: Adding an `alias` line to `~/.bashrc` makes it available in every new interactive shell session, since that file is sourced automatically on shell startup.

title: Write a systemd service unit
prompt: Write a systemd unit file that runs `/opt/app/start.sh` as user `appuser`, automatically restarts on failure, and starts after the network is up.

correctAnswer.solution:
[Unit]
Description=My Application
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=appuser
ExecStart=/opt/app/start.sh
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[WantedBy]=multi-user.target

explanation: `After=network.target` orders startup relative to networking without hard-requiring it succeed first. `Restart=on-failure` plus `RestartSec=5` gives systemd's own crash-recovery instead of relying on an external supervisor, and `Type=simple` tells systemd the ExecStart process itself is the main service process to track.

title: Query systemd logs by time range and priority
prompt: Write a journalctl command to show only ERROR-level-or-worse logs from the `app.service` unit in the last 2 hours.
explanation: `-u` filters to a specific systemd unit, `-p err` includes that priority and everything more severe (crit, alert, emerg), and `--since` accepts natural-language relative time expressions.

title: Parallel-process a list of files
prompt: Write a command that resizes every `.png` file under `/data/images` to 800px wide using `convert`, processing up to 4 files at once in parallel.
explanation: `-P4` tells xargs to run up to 4 invocations of the command concurrently instead of one at a time, and `-I{}` lets the found filename be substituted into both the input and output position of the `convert` command — a common way to parallelize a slow per-file batch job without writing a script.
